title: Pre-LA piece-to-camera — 'Nine days. LA. Five questions.'
coreArgument: Tim coming to LA with questions, not a pitch deck, signals operator-confidence and surfaces inbound from people who want to meet — and being on camera (not just writing) is what a vertical-native operator looks like in 2026.
whyNow: LA trip Jun 1–4 is 9 days out. The window to set the narrative BEFORE Tim lands is closing. Pre-trip piece-to-camera turns a private calendar item into a public agenda before he walks into a single meeting.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed LA meetings trip Jun 1–4 (data/state/speaking.json). Tim's weekly focus: 'Build LA trip momentum.' knowledge/VOICE.md → On-camera voice rules.
timPOV: Hollywood keeps asking the wrong question. The interesting question isn't 'is vertical real?' — that was 2024. The interesting question is what gets built on top of it.
colRelevance: Frames Tim as the operator Hollywood is coming to, not the operator selling Hollywood. Surfaces inbound for COL's Microdrama-in-a-Box B2B narrative ahead of meetings. Visual native = COL native.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–3 sec):** Tim outside — Singapore daylight, not at a desk. Phone at chest height. Camera straight on.
**Beat 1 (3–10 sec):** 'Nine days. LA. Five questions.' Hold beat. No music.

**Beat 2 (10–35 sec):** Three of the five questions on camera, fast — '(1) Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical? (2) Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy? (3) Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item, not a stunt?' Two more in the caption.
**Beat 3 (35–50 sec):** 'I'm not coming to LA to sell anything. I'm coming to find out what people are actually doing when nobody's posting about it.'
**Close (50–60 sec):** 'I'll tell you what I find.' Forward line, no sign-off. Cut.
**Caption (~90 words):** Restate the hook, list ALL five questions, end with 'Who should I be asking?' — generates inbound.
risk: Tim sounding like he's auditioning Hollywood. Mitigation: shot OUTSIDE a corporate setting (no COL-branded backdrop), no suit jacket, energy not gravitas. Watch the cuts for swagger.
body:
[SHOOT INSTRUCTIONS]
Location: Singapore daylight, somewhere with depth in the background — not a desk, not a logo wall. A street, a hotel lobby, the FlareFlow studio entrance.
Wardrobe: white tee or open-collar shirt. NO suit jacket. NO tie.
Phone at chest height, vertical, native camera app. Single take. Allow 3–4 attempts.
Audio: phone mic is fine. If outdoors, find a wall to break wind noise.

[ON-CAMERA SCRIPT — ~60 seconds total]

(Direct, energy, half a beat more confident than written-Tim. No 'hey everyone'. No 'so'.)

Nine days. LA. Five questions.

(Beat.)

One. Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical right now — and which are still pretending not to.

Two. Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy that isn't just "we'll repackage TikTok talent."

Three. Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item — not a one-off stunt.

(Two more in the caption.)

I'm not coming to LA to sell anything. I'm coming to find out what people are actually doing when nobody's posting about it.

I'll tell you what I find.

[CAPTION TEXT — for LinkedIn / X post body]

Nine days. LA. Five questions I'm going to ask everyone.

1. Which production execs are quietly piloting vertical right now — and which are still pretending not to?

2. Which agencies have a real creator-vertical strategy, not just "we'll repackage TikTok talent"?

3. Which brand CMOs are buying vertical drama as a media line item — not a one-off stunt?

4. Which platforms are licensing vertical IP vs commissioning it?

5. What does the next CAA / WME signing in vertical look like — and from where?

Who should I be asking?
rationale: Hook in 5 words — passes the VOICE.md on-camera test. Outdoor, phone, no jacket — passes the 'not a corporate piece' test. Specific questions with named institutions (CAA, WME, brand CMOs) — passes the 'no fluff' test. Forward close ('I'll tell you what I find') sets up the LA daily vlog series — building cadence. Generates inbound from the caption's closing question. This replaces last week's written version of the same idea.

title: Day 1 LA travel vlog — beat sheet (record Jun 1)
hook: Day one. LA. Here's the list.
coreArgument: Daily ~60-second packages from LA build cadence + agenda — turn a four-day private trip into four public artefacts that compound across the rest of the LA-week posts.
whyNow: Tim lands LA Jun 1. Day-1 beat needs to be shot ON Jun 1 — not from notes after the fact. The cadence is the value; one daily post Jun 1–Jun 4 is the deliverable.
sourceEvidence: Confirmed LA trip Jun 1–4 (data/state/speaking.json). knowledge/OPPORTUNITY_RADAR.md → Category 21 Visual content / travel vlog beats. knowledge/VOICE.md → 'Always shoot when travelling.'
timPOV: Most exec travel content is either 'arrived in city, excited to meet team' (corporate, dead) or 'look at the view from my hotel' (extractive, dead). The version that lands: name the people, name the questions, name the day. Specific beats specificity.
colRelevance: Builds Tim's recognisability in LA / Hollywood B-roll. Establishes 'Tim shows up' as a brand-level expectation — recurrence is part of mogul-construction.
supportingPoints: **Open (0–4 sec):** Walking shot. LAX exit OR Sunset Blvd OR Studio gates. 'Day one. LA. Here's the list.' Hold.
**Beat 1 (4–20 sec):** Name 3 meetings publicly (only if confirmed + non-confidential). Format: 'Today: [name / role], [name / role], [name / role].' Direct to camera. Walking.
**Beat 2 (20–40 sec):** Frame for the day. 'The question I'm bringing: [one specific question — e.g. "What does a brand-CMO want from vertical that they're not getting?"].'
**Beat 3 (40–55 sec):** One overheard detail / first impression. 'Already noticed: [thing]. We'll see if that holds.' (No spin, just observation.)
**Close (55–60 sec):** 'Tomorrow.' Cut.
**Caption (~50 words):** Recap the question. Tag the people if their names were used on camera (with their permission). End with 'Day 2 tomorrow.'
**Same beat sheet for Days 2, 3, 4** — substitute meetings + question + observation. Tim's job is to film 60 seconds per day, not to script a series. Stuart caption-edits + posts.
risk: Naming meetings that haven't given permission to be named publicly. Mitigation: ONLY name people Stuart has cleared. Default to 'studio exec' / 'agency partner' if no clearance. Risk #2: Tim's energy flags by Day 3. Mitigation: shoot Day 3 in the morning, not after a 9pm dinner.
body:
[BEAT SHEET — not a script. Tim improvises within the structure each day.]

DAY 1 — JUN 1 (record between landing + first meeting)

Location options (pick what feels native): LAX exit (the wheelchair luggage moment), arriving at the hotel, Sunset Blvd walking shot, studio lot gates (if visiting one).

Opening: 'Day one. LA. Here's the list.'

Meetings to name (Stuart confirms permission for each name on the morning of Jun 1):
  • Meeting 1 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'studio exec at [studio]')
  • Meeting 2 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'agency partner')
  • Meeting 3 — [name / role] (or fallback: 'investor — consumer + China thesis')

Question for the day (Tim picks ONE the night before):
  • Option A: 'What does a brand CMO want from vertical that they're not getting?'
  • Option B: 'Which agency has a vertical-native creator desk first — and what would they want from us?'
  • Option C: 'What's the moment Hollywood actually says yes to vertical at scale?'

Overheard / first impression:
  • Whatever lands. Authentic > clever.

Close: 'Tomorrow.' One word. Cut.

[DAY 2 — JUN 2]
Same structure. New meetings. New question (don't repeat).
Location: morning coffee, between two meetings, Beverly Hills sidewalk — wherever.

[DAY 3 — JUN 3]
Same structure. By now Tim should be naming patterns ('Three people have asked me the same question. That tells me...'). Higher value than Day 1.

[DAY 4 — JUN 4]
Final day = recap variant. 'Four days, fifteen meetings, here's what changed my mind.' Slightly longer (~75 sec OK). This is the post that gets reposted — make it the sharpest.

[CAPTION TEMPLATE — same for each day]

Day [N] in LA. Today's question: [the question]. Met: [up to 3 names if cleared].

[One-line observation, copied from the video close.]

Day [N+1] tomorrow.

[POSTING CADENCE]
Daily, between 6pm–9pm LA time (which is the LinkedIn-APAC-morning sweet spot for Tim's home audience + still primetime in US). Stuart edits captions + posts; Tim's only job is to film 60 seconds per day.
rationale: Beat sheet, not a script — Tim improvises within structure, which is more native to vertical-creator energy. Forces ONE question per day = surfaces the LA conversation as it unfolds. Daily cadence = compounding visibility, not one-off. Caption template + clearance protocol = removes Stuart-side friction during the actual trip. Day 4 designed as the share-able recap. This is the kind of asset that makes Tim recognisable in Hollywood B-roll — Category 21 in practice.

title: The Vertical View — issue #1 launch announcement
hook: Six months of writing this in my head. Time to publish it.
coreArgument: Vertical content needs a serious, operator-led publication that treats it as a real category — not a TikTok think-piece. Tim is the person to launch it, because he's the only English-language operator running it at scale.
whyNow: Weekly focus explicitly lists 'Launch Vertical View newsletter'. Quarter goal: cement Vertical 2.0 framing in press. Owning a publication is how you stop being a subject of coverage and start being the source. (Parked from this week's 'Act on this week' surface to respect the 3-item time budget — but kept in drafts for next-week consideration.)
sourceEvidence: Quarter goals in data/state/settings.json. Tim's coverage in TVBIZZ, ContentAsia, SCMP, Vitrina LeaderSpeak (data/state/press.json) demonstrates ongoing trade-press demand.
timPOV: Most writing about microdrama is either chart-of-the-week or 'is this real?' takes. Neither is useful to anyone building. The Vertical View will be the version I'd want to read on a Sunday morning.
colRelevance: Direct owned-media channel for COL signals — investor narrative, partnership announcements, hiring, market data. Removes COL's dependence on inbound trade coverage for narrative control.
supportingPoints: Issue cadence: every other Sunday. Operator depth, not news rehash.
Coverage scope: vertical drama, mobile-first storytelling, brand entry, Asia-to-global content flow, AI in production.
Format: 1 long-read + 1 numbered list + 3 things to read this week. No fluff.
Audience target: people who run things — production, distribution, brand, investment. Not 'creators' as a generic bucket.
First-issue topic: why the Australian payment rate (20%) is the most underread data point in entertainment right now.
risk: Newsletter starts strong then dies. Mitigation: pre-write three issues before launch, schedule a fourth, hand cadence to Stuart for accountability.
body:
Six months ago I started writing one of these in my head every Sunday morning.

Today I'm publishing it.

It's called The Vertical View. Every other Sunday. One long-read, one numbered list, three things to read this week. Operator depth, not news rehash.

Why now: the industry has hit the point where there are more think-pieces than people who can tell you what a 77-minute session looks like in the data.

Why me: I've spent the last year as the only English-language exec running global vertical distribution at scale. 1,700+ titles across 10+ markets. 2.4B+ views. I'm not pretending it's easy. I'm saying it deserves serious writing.

Issue #1 next Sunday: why the Australian payment rate at 20% — more than double most developed markets — is the most underread data point in entertainment right now.

Link in comments. Subscribe if you want this in your inbox. Forward to one person who'd find it useful — that's how I'll know if it works.
rationale: Opens personal, lands the public commitment, sets cadence so accountability is built in. Uses Tim's actual numbers (1,700 / 10 / 2.4B / 77 / 20%). One idea per sentence. Ends with a soft ask (forward to one person) — Tim's natural humility frame. Holds until next week per the time-budget cap.

title: Australia at 20% — the underread number
hook: Australia is converting at 20%. Nobody in this industry is talking about it.
coreArgument: Australia's new-user payment rate at ~20% — more than double most developed markets — disproves the lazy assumption that vertical drama is a developing-market phenomenon. Developed markets are converting harder, not softer.
whyNow: Tim revealed this data at Cairns Crocodiles on May 19. The number deserves a wider second life on LinkedIn — first time it's been published in his own voice rather than via panel coverage.
sourceEvidence: Cairns Crocodiles speaking record (data/state/speaking.json). Australia data was disclosed at the 'Maestros of Microdramas' session with Nikyah Hutchings.
timPOV: If you believed the consensus that vertical drama was a Tier 2/3-market phenomenon, the Australia number breaks your model. The lazy framing — 'it works because people in emerging markets have small phones' — collapses when developed-market conversion is higher than emerging-market conversion.
colRelevance: Establishes COL as the operator with the data — the trade outlets and analysts (Maria Rua Aguete, Omdia; Atul Phadnis, Vitrina) cite Tim because he has numbers nobody else has.
supportingPoints: Australia new-user payment rate: ~20%. More than 2x most developed markets.
Counterfactual: if vertical was a developing-market phenomenon, you'd expect inverse — Australia would be a laggard.

Why it's actually true: in developed markets, time-poor consumers value formats that respect their time. 77-minute sessions on FlareFlow prove the demand was always there for short-attention narrative.
Implication: brand spend follows conversion, not impressions. Australia's payment rate is a brand-CMO signal more than a creator signal.
risk: Single-market datapoint gets framed as cherry-picked. Mitigation: name the rate explicitly as a single market, note Tim is asking why — not asserting it generalises.
body:
Australia is converting at 20%.

New-user payment rate. More than double most developed markets.

Nobody in this industry is talking about it. Most takes still assume vertical drama is a developing-market phenomenon — small phones, small budgets, small attention spans.

The Australia number breaks that frame.

If the consensus were right, Australia would be a laggard. It's the opposite. Developed-market viewers — time-poor, format-literate, brand-saturated — are paying *more* readily, not less.

Which means the question stops being "will vertical work in developed markets?" and starts being "what's the brand-CMO going to do about it?"

Because brand spend follows conversion, not impressions.

I'm not saying Australia generalises. I'm saying it's the most underread number in entertainment right now.

Who's seeing similar in another developed market — would love a quiet DM.
rationale: Opens with the headline number. One idea per sentence. Names the consensus, breaks it, reframes the implication for the audience Tim wants to reach (brand CMOs). Closes with a DM ask — Tim's standard relationship-building move. No hashtags, no emoji.
